evolved_hypotheses.title: TST Dissolution Kinetics in the Surface-Reaction-Limited Regime of Low Drug-Loading ASDs
evolved_hypotheses.improvement_summary: Resolves critic's primary objection by identifying the specific ASD dissolution regime where TST applies (Da << 1, low drug loading < 20 wt%), naming the rate-limiting molecular event (drug-polymer H-bond disruption at the interface, Ea = 65-85 kJ/mol), providing a Damköhler number criterion for regime identification, predicting the Temkin coefficient (σ = 0.30-0.40 for indomethacin-HPMCAS from H-bond stoichiometry), predicting a drug-loading crossover at ~25 wt%, and providing a hard 1-number falsification test (Ea < 35 kJ/mol at 10% loading kills hypothesis).
key_predictions: 10 wt% indomethacin-HPMCAS: Ea = 65-80 kJ/mol (surface-reaction-limited, TST applies)
40 wt% indomethacin-HPMCAS: Ea = 15-30 kJ/mol (diffusion-limited, TST inapplicable)
Crossover at ~25 wt% drug loading where Da ≈ 1
σ = 0.30-0.40 for indomethacin-HPMCAS (derived from ~3 H-bonds per drug molecule)
TST curve fit R² > 0.95 for 10% loading at varied C_drug/C_am ratios
evolved_hypotheses.bridge: TST activation energy (Ea) for drug-polymer H-bond disruption at ASD-water interface, operative under low-Damköhler (Da << 1) surface-reaction-limited conditions
evolved_hypotheses.diversity_check: UNIQUE bridge: TST activation energy with H-bond disruption molecular event under Damköhler criterion. Distinct from H1.2-E (RL-2 ODE passivation-erosion) and H1.6-E (dual-SI phase competition)

evolved_hypotheses.title: Grambow Rate Law 2 Predicts Competitive Passivation-Erosion Kinetics and Regime Switching in ASD Dissolution
evolved_hypotheses.improvement_summary: Replaces Grambow 1985 Rate Law 1 (permanent passivation layer) with Grambow-Müller 2001 Rate Law 2 (competitive passivation-erosion ODE), which explicitly handles polymer layer erosion — the critic's primary objection. The RL-2 ODE (dh/dt = α·D_drug/h − β·k_erase) predicts three dissolution regimes (parabolic, zero-order, erosion-controlled) as a function of polymer molecular weight. Assigns molecular sources for D_drug (free-volume theory) and k_erase (reptation theory, MW^-3.5 scaling). Introduces QCM-D as independent verification of k_erase. PVP-VA negative control distinguishes passivation mechanism from generic Higuchi kinetics. Corrects Gin et al. journal (Nature Comm, not Nature Materials).
key_predictions: HPMCAS-H (330 kDa): parabolic release (G << 1, √t kinetics, R² > 0.92)
HPMCAS-M (78 kDa): ~zero-order release after initial burst (G ~ 1, steady-state layer)
k_erase scales as MW^-3.5 across three HPMCAS grades (within factor of 3)
D_drug from RL-2 dissolution fit agrees with QCM-D k_erase within one order of magnitude
PVP-VA control: no MW-dependent regime switching (Higuchi control, not passivation)
evolved_hypotheses.bridge: Grambow-Müller 2001 Rate Law 2 competitive passivation-erosion ODE from nuclear waste borosilicate glass dissolution regulatory modeling, applied to ASD polymer-rich surface layer dynamics
evolved_hypotheses.diversity_check: UNIQUE bridge: Grambow RL-2 competitive passivation-erosion ODE with reptation-theory k_erase. Distinct from H1.1-E (TST surface kinetics) and H1.6-E (thermodynamic dual-SI competition)

evolved_hypotheses.title: Dual Saturation Index Competition Predicts LLPS vs. Crystallization Pathway Switching in Ionizable Drug ASD Dissolution
evolved_hypotheses.improvement_summary: Resolves critic's primary objection that SI = log(a/a_eq) is equivalent to MFAD 2019. Evolution identifies the genuine novelty: geochemical speciation codes compute saturation indices for ALL POSSIBLE PHASES SIMULTANEOUSLY, which MFAD 2019 cannot do (MFAD tracks only crystalline reference). The dual-SI framework (SI_LLPS and SI_cryst computed in parallel) applies Ostwald's Rule of Stages to predict WHICH precipitation pathway occurs first — LLPS (preserves supersaturation, beneficial) or crystallization (destroys supersaturation, harmful). Provides pH_crit formula for ionizable drugs, specific quantitative prediction for posaconazole at pH 1.2 and 6.8, PC-SAFT as the activity coefficient model, and a 12-condition (3 drugs × 4 pH) falsification matrix.
key_predictions: For posaconazole at pH 6.8: DLS detects LLPS at 1.5 μg/mL, ≥15 min before PXRD detects crystallization
For posaconazole at pH 1.2: neither LLPS nor crystallization at therapeutic concentrations (SI_LLPS and SI_cryst both negative)
pH 'dangerous window' (pH 4-5 for posaconazole): LLPS and crystallization concurrent (<5 min lag)
Dual-SI correctly predicts LLPS-first vs. concurrent vs. cryst-first sequence in ≥9/12 conditions
Sequence prediction is pH-dependent for ionizable drugs (pH-independent for non-ionizable controls)
evolved_hypotheses.bridge: Multi-phase simultaneous saturation index tracking from geochemical speciation (SI_LLPS vs. SI_cryst computed in parallel via Ostwald's Rule of Stages), predicting LLPS/crystallization pathway sequence as a function of pH for ionizable drugs
evolved_hypotheses.diversity_check: UNIQUE bridge: Dual-SI multi-phase competition (SI_LLPS vs. SI_cryst) from geochemical speciation applied to pH-dependent precipitation pathway selection. Distinct from H1.1-E (surface kinetics) and H1.2-E (layer dynamics ODE)

excluded.reason: Reduces to QSPR with extra steps; dimensionality problem (drug structural diversity >> silicate oxide space) has no evolutionary solution. Elo tournament confirmed 0/3 wins. Not evolved.

diversity_bridges: H1.1-E: TST activation energy / Damköhler criterion / surface kinetics
H1.2-E: Grambow RL-2 ODE / competitive passivation-erosion / reptation theory
H1.6-E: Dual-SI multi-phase speciation / Ostwald Rule of Stages / pH-dependent pathway
evolution_quality_check: All three evolved hypotheses are substantively stronger than their parents. H1.1-E resolves the critic's regime question with a quantitative criterion (Damköhler number) and named molecular event (H-bond disruption, Ea prediction). H1.2-E replaces a permanent-passivation analogy with a competitive ODE that handles erosion, adds reptation-theory molecular sources, and provides a negative control to distinguish the mechanism from generic Higuchi kinetics. H1.6-E transforms a MFAD-equivalent repackaging into a genuinely new dual-pathway prediction tool using simultaneous SI competition — MFAD 2019 cannot predict LLPS vs. crystallization sequence. No two hypotheses share a bridge mechanism. No incoherent crossovers. Largest improvements in MechSpec (H1.2-E: 4→8) and Novelty/Impact (H1.6-E: 3→7 for both). H1.1-E remains the composite leader with estimated 8.4.
